Document Title: A Reasoning System for Graded Modal Logic
Author Affiliations: IIMAS: Posgrado en Ciencia e lngeniería de la Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingenería: Dpto. de Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 12th International Conference in Software Engineering Research and Innovation (CONISOFT)
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a deduction system for the modal logic $\mathbf{K}$ with graded modalities, which is obtained by adding numerical constraints to the modal operators. Graded modalities can be seen as a generalization of traditional modal operators since they include numerical bounds in the modalities, thus enabling the formal treatment of more detailed expressions, such as “there are at least $n+1$ individuals satisfying a certain property”, where $n$ is a natural number. This kind of properties commonly arise in contexts such as concurrent systems: “if at least 3 processors are free, then proceed to compute the next task”, or epistemic logic:”if more than 2 people know $P$, then $P$ is known”. The system is based on the notion of tree-hypersequents. We show the system is correct, i.e. sound and complete.
IEEE Terms: Technological innovation;Interpolation;Program processors;Computational modeling;Syntactics;Cognition;Numerical models;Logic;Iterative methods;Formal verification

Document Title: Transforming Software Development: A Study on the Integration of Multi-Agent Systems and Large Language Models for Automatic Code Generation
Authors: R. Ramírez-Rueda; E. Benítez-Guerrero; C. Mezura-Godoy; E. Bárcenas
Author Affiliations: Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad veracruzana, Xalapa, Mexico; Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad veracruzana, Xalapa, Mexico; Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad veracruzana, Xalapa, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, México
Publication Title: 2024 12th International Conference in Software Engineering Research and Innovation (CONISOFT)
Abstract: This paper explores the integration of Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) and Large Language Models (LLMs) for auto-matic code generation, addressing the limitations of traditional manual coding. By conducting a comprehensive review of existing literature and analyzing a practical case study, we demonstrate how MAS and LLMs can collaboratively enhance software development processes. The research focuses on the technical and theoretical challenges of this integration, highlighting the potential for improved productivity, adaptability, and quality in code generation. The findings contribute to AI-based software engineering by revealing new research directions in collective intelligence and automated programming.
IEEE Terms: Productivity;Technological innovation;Codes;Large language models;Collective intelligence;Software;Standards;Software development management;Software engineering;Multi-agent systems

Document Title: Keep it Simple: Understanding Natural Language Commands for General-Purpose Service Robots
Authors: S. Ortuno-Chanelo; L. Contreras; J. Savage; H. Okada
Author Affiliations: Biorobotics Laboratory, School of Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico; Advance Intelligence and Robotics Research Center, Tamagawa University; Biorobotics Laboratory, School of Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico; Advance Intelligence and Robotics Research Center, Tamagawa University
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE/SICE International Symposium on System Integration (SII)
Abstract: Service robots are designed to perform useful tasks for humans, which involve managing and combining a variety of skills in the form of global and local plans to solve any given task. In this work, we propose a framework to process natural language commands for general-purpose service robots where the robot should perform an arbitrary spoken command requested by a non-expert operator. Our system uses a Natural Language Processing (NLP) parser and a Conceptual Dependency (CD) builder to create CD structures that an expert system can employ to generate a global plan that the robot will execute. An extra simplification step using Large Language Models (LLM) was tested and evaluated in order to improve the accuracy of the system. Finally, our system has been tested in challenging environments in robot competitions and we have achieved promising results.
IEEE Terms: Service robots;System integration;Syntactics;Natural language processing;Generators;Task analysis;Expert systems
